variables.content: Because direct thermodynamic-limit extrapolation of the discrete-angle DMC-determined Fermi liquid parameters proved unreliable, a set of “best available” numerical recommendations for the Fermi liquid parameters was assembled by combining independently published QMC results for related quantities: quasiparticle effective masses $m^{*}$ obtained from DMC energy-band calculations and smooth QMC-based parameterizations of the ground-state correlation energy.  Using the literature effective masses together with compressibility and spin-susceptibility values derived from the total-energy parameterizations and the Landau relations yields recommended values such as, for the paramagnetic 2D HEG: at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}^{s}=-0.495(2)$, $F_{0}^{a}=-0.346(2)$, $F_{1}^{s}=-0.053(3)$; at $r_{s}=5$ and $10$ analogous recommended values are provided; and for the ferromagnetic 2D HEG recommended values include at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}=-0.428(2)$ and $\bar{F}_1=-0.159(3)$.  These assembled values combine ground-state energy parameterizations and published effective masses to provide pragmatic numerical recommendations when direct thermodynamic-limit determinations of $F_l$ from discrete-angle DMC data are unreliable.

The quasiparticle effective masses m^{*} reported in the literature from DMC fits to the single-particle energy band and the ground-state total-energy parametrizations reported in the literature as continuous functions E_{\mathrm{corr}}(r_s,\zeta) of density parameter r_s and spin polarization \zeta are mutually consistent within their stated uncertainties and can be combined algebraically via standard Fermi-liquid thermodynamic relations (for example F_1^{s} = m^{*}-1 for the paramagnetic HEG and evaluation of F_0^{s,a} from derivatives of E(r_s,\zeta)) to produce numerical estimates of the dimensionless Fermi liquid parameters F_l for the 2D HEG.
      Refs. 11,23,24

The proposition: Parameterizations of the ground-state total energy per electron $E(r_s,\zeta)$ obtained from independent quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) studies (specifically the smooth correlation-energy parameterizations reported in the literature) produce isothermal compressibility ratios $\kappa/\kappa^*$ and spin-susceptibility ratios $\chi/\chi^*$ via the total-energy curvature formulas
      $$
      \frac{\kappa}{\kappa^{*}}=\frac{r_{s}^{4}}{4(1+\zeta^{2})}\left[\frac{\partial^{2}}{\partial r_{s}^{2}}-\frac{1}{r_{s}}\frac{\partial}{\partial r_{s}}\right]E(r_{s},\zeta)
      $$
      and
      $$
      \frac{\chi}{\chi^{*}}=r_{s}^{2}\left(\frac{\partial^{2}E}{\partial\zeta^{2}}\right)_{\zeta=0},
      $$
      that are accurate to within the quoted parameterization uncertainties and are mutually consistent across the two independent parameterizations, so that disagreements with compressibility and susceptibility values computed from extrapolated Fermi liquid parameters are likely attributable to deficiencies in the finite-size-extrapolated Fermi liquid parameters rather than to errors in these total-energy parameterizations.
      Ref. 23; Ref. 24

The proposition: Taking quasiparticle effective masses $m^{*}$ reported in prior DMC energy-band calculations (for example, numerical values reported in the literature) and combining them with isothermal compressibility and spin-susceptibility ratios computed from QMC-based ground-state total-energy parameterizations, then solving the Landau relations
      $$
      \frac{\kappa}{\kappa^{*}}=\frac{1}{m^{*}}+\frac{F_{0}^{s}}{m^{*}},\qquad
      \frac{\chi}{\chi^{*}}=\frac{1}{m^{*}}+\frac{F_{0}^{a}}{m^{*}},\qquad
      m^{*}=1+F_{1}^{s}\ \text{(paramagnetic) or}\ m^{*}=1+F_{1}\ \text{(ferromagnetic)},
      $$
      yields numerical values of the Fermi liquid parameters $F_0^{s}$, $F_0^{a}$, $F_1^{s}$ (paramagnetic) and $F_0$, $F_1$ (ferromagnetic) that serve as pragmatic “best available” recommendations when direct thermodynamic-limit determinations of $F_l$ from discrete-angle DMC data are unreliable; here all quantities are evaluated at the same density parameter $r_s$ and spin polarization $\zeta$.
      Ref. 11

Comparisons between thermodynamic quantities computed from the extrapolated Fermi liquid parameters and the same quantities obtained directly from smooth parameterizations of ground-state total DMC energies reveal substantial numerical disagreement.  Explicitly, the isothermal compressibility ratio computed from Fermi-liquid relations,
      $$
      \frac{\kappa}{\kappa^{*}}=\frac{1}{m^{*}}+\frac{F_{0}^{s}}{m^{*}}\quad\text{(paramagnetic)}
      $$
      or
      $$
      \frac{\kappa}{\kappa^{*}}=\frac{1}{m^{*}}+\frac{F_{0}}{m^{*}}\quad\text{(ferromagnetic)},
      $$
      and the isothermal spin-susceptibility ratio computed from
      $$
      \frac{\chi}{\chi^{*}}=\frac{1}{m^{*}}+\frac{F_{0}^{a}}{m^{*}},
      $$
      differ substantially from the values obtained by evaluating curvature formulas of the ground-state total-energy per electron $E(r_s,\zeta)$, namely
      $$
      \frac{\kappa}{\kappa^{*}}=\frac{r_s^4}{4(1+\zeta^2)}\Big[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial r_s^2}-\frac{1}{r_s}\frac{\partial}{\partial r_s}\Big]E(r_s,\zeta)
      $$
      and
      $$
      \frac{\chi}{\chi^{*}}=r_s^2\Big(\frac{\partial^2 E}{\partial\zeta^2}\Big)_{\zeta=0},
      $$
      where $\zeta$ denotes the spin polarization and $m^{*}$ the quasiparticle effective mass.  Because the total-energy curvature estimates obtained from smooth parameterizations of ground-state DMC energies are internally consistent, the observed discrepancies indicate that the extrapolated Fermi liquid parameters obtained from the discrete-angle DMC data and finite-size extrapolations are not numerically reliable determinations of the thermodynamic-limit Fermi liquid parameters.

Published quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) total-energy data for the homogeneous Fermi liquid (spin-polarized and unpolarized) serve as accurate benchmarks for the liquid-phase ground-state energy per electron as a function of $r_{s}$ and can be directly compared to the harmonic-level crystalline energies computed here, taking into account the reported QMC statistical and systematic uncertainties.

Two independent procedures for evaluating thermodynamic response ratios produce significantly different results for the paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas: (i) the total-energy approach, which evaluates the isothermal compressibility ratio \kappa/\kappa^{*} and the spin-susceptibility ratio \chi/\chi^{*} directly from parametrizations of the ground-state total energy per electron E(r_s,\zeta) reported in the literature, using formulas such as
      \kappa/\kappa^{*} = \frac{r_s^4}{4(1+\zeta^2)}\Bigl[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial r_s^2} - \frac{1}{r_s}\frac{\partial}{\partial r_s}\Bigr] E(r_s,\zeta)
      and
      \chi/\chi^{*} = r_s^2\Bigl(\frac{\partial^2 E}{\partial \zeta^2}\Bigr)_{\zeta=0},
      and (ii) the Fermi-liquid approach, which computes the same ratios from the Fermi liquid relations
      \kappa/\kappa^{*} = 1/m^{*} + F_0^{s}/m^{*},\qquad \chi/\chi^{*} = 1/m^{*} + F_0^{a}/m^{*},
      using the extrapolated F_0^{s}/m^{*} and F_0^{a}/m^{*} reported above together with independently determined effective masses m^{*}. These two approaches give markedly different numerical values: for example, for the paramagnetic r_s = 5 system the total-energy approach yields \kappa/\kappa^{*} = -1.735 and \chi/\chi^{*} = 0.296, whereas the Fermi-liquid approach using the extrapolated Fermi liquid parameters and literature effective masses yields \kappa/\kappa^{*} = 1.7(8) and \chi/\chi^{*} = 3.0(7). These discrepancies indicate that the extrapolated Fermi liquid parameters obtained from the finite-cell QMC Landau-function procedure are not quantitatively consistent with ground-state total-energy parametrizations.

When the HEG limit ($\nabla\rho=0$) of the model's coupling-constant potential expressions $V_{\mathrm{C}}^{\sigma\sigma',\lambda=1}$ is fitted by least-squares to reference Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) values of $V_{\mathrm{C}}^{\sigma\sigma',\lambda=1}$ across metallic densities $r_{\mathrm{s}}=1,2,\dots,10$, the best-fit constants are $F_{\alpha\beta}=2.1070$ and $F_{\alpha\alpha}=2.6422$, and the mean absolute percentage errors (MAPE) of the fit relative to the QMC reference are 5.0% for the opposite-spin component and 12.0% for the parallel-spin component, yielding a total $V_{\mathrm{C}}^{\lambda=1}$ MAPE of 4.6%; these numerical fitting outcomes were obtained by fitting the model HEG expressions to QMC-derived $V_{\mathrm{C}}^{\sigma\sigma',\lambda=1}$ values.
      P. Gori-Giorgi, F. Sacchetti, and G. Bachelet, Phys. Rev. B 61, 7353 (2000) [QMC HEG reference data]

Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.
